Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas Kabriil Khajryan a,* , Robert Hoffmann b, Thomas Storch a,1

, Thomas Grab a

, Robert Kircheisen b, Olga Ravkina b

, Ralf Kriegel b , Tobias Fieback a a Chair of Technical Thermodynamics, Institute of Thermal Engineering, TU Bergakademie Freiberg, Gustav-Zeuner-Straße 7, 09599, Freiberg, Germanyb Fraunhofer Institute for Ceramic Technologies and Systems IKTS, High-Temperature Separation and Catalysis, Michael-Faraday-Straße 1, 07629, Hermsdorf, Germany

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Decentral oxygen productionMixed ionic-electronic conductorBSCF membraneSteam sweep gasWaste heat utilization

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: A B S T R A C T A decentralized and thermal method for oxygen production is very beneficial for smalland medium-scale consumers, especially for industries that generate significant amounts of high-temperature waste heat, such as steel and glassworks. Currently, the majority of oxygen production is achieved with central cryogenic air sep­aration units. Other fully developed decentralized methods, such as (vacuum) pressure swing adsorption, have a high electrical energy demand and achieve a maximum oxygen purity of 95 vol%. This study offers an alternative utilizing mixed ionic-electronic conductor membranes made of Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ to separate oxygen from air using steam as a sweep gas. When heated above 700 ◦C, these membranes can separate high-purity oxygen using the oxygen partial pressure difference between air and steam. To investigate the functionality of this method, an experimental setup utilizing nine monolithic membrane tubes was developed. During the 7-h-long experiments, the furnace temperature was varied in the range from 700 ◦C to 900 ◦C, the air flow rate from 0 L/min to 12 L/min and the mass flow rate of steam from 200 g/h to 500 g/h. Results exhibited an approximately linear increase in oxygen output with furnace temperature and a higher oxygen separation degree at elevated steam mass flow rates. Therefore, a maximum oxygen flow rate of 960 mL/min with a purity of 99 vol% was achieved at 900 ◦C and a steam mass flow of 500 g/h.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: mass flow rates. Therefore, a maximum oxygen flow rate of 960 mL/min with a purity of 99 vol% was achieved at 900 ◦C and a steam mass flow of 500 g/h. The remaining impurities were mainly non-condensed water vapor. With implemented smart heat management, these results show a promising outlook for the process.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: 1. Introduction The production of high-purity oxygen is of vital significance, as ox­ygen is used in many industries, for example, in steelworks, glass manufacturing, and brick production. The global consumption of oxy­gen has increased from 99.1 billion m3/a STP in 2010 to 117.2 billion m3/a STP in 2020 [1]. Most of this demand is produced using centralized cryogenic air separation units (ASUs), followed by distribution to end users. However, for small-to medium-scale consumers, decentralized oxygen production is more beneficial due to the elimination of transport and storage costs. On-site oxygen generation also facilitates processes like oxy-fuel combustion, accompanied by the saving of primary fuel [2] and a flue gas consisting mainly of CO2 [3]. Current technologies, such as pressure swing adsorption (PSA) or vacuum pressure swing adsorp­tion (VPSA) plants, are commonly used for this purpose. However, they are limited to a maximum O2 purity of 95 vol%, while achieving higher purities is only possible at much greater costs [4] and comes with high electrical energy demand. A brief overview of these technologies is presented in Table 1. An alternative approach is based on oxygen-permeable ceramic membranes, namely mixed ionic-electronic conductors (MIECs) that selectively separate oxygen from air. Among these, a promising type is the mixed oxide membrane with perovskite structure, crystallizing in a cubic structure based on the general formula ABO3-δ.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: these, a promising type is the mixed oxide membrane with perovskite structure, crystallizing in a cubic structure based on the general formula ABO3-δ. “A” represents ions with a larger radius (e.g., Ca, Sr, Ba, etc.) and “B” represents transition metals (e.g., Fe, Al, Co, etc.). The oxygen nonstoichiometry parameter “δ” depends on the temperature and partial pressure of oxygen and characterizes the concentration of oxygen vacancies within the crystal lattice. The different oxygen partial pressures at both membrane sur­faces will create a gradient of oxygen vacancy concentration across the

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: Available online 15 November 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). membrane thickness. If the temperature is high enough to increase the ion mobility, oxygen permeation is reached [8]. At first, Teraoka et al. [9] demonstrated that substituting the “A” and “B” ions with mixed elements improves oxygen vacancies and phase stability of the membranes. Thus, compositions like LaxSr1-xCoyFe1-yO3-δ (LSCF) [10,11] or BaxSr1-xCoyFe1-yO3-δ (BSCF) [12–15] became of in­terest. While many studies investigate flat membranes [11,16,17], membrane tubes are much better suited for industrial applications due to reduced mechanical stress [18,19]. These membrane tubes can be monolithic [11,18,20], capillary [14,21], or asymmetric [22,23]. The latter features a thin, gas-tight layer of ceramic material on a thick-walled porous support for increased mechanical stability of the whole tube. Due to the thinner walls of the solid material, capillary and asymmetric membrane tubes possess improved oxygen permeability. However, monolithic membranes remain cheaper and easier to produce [18,19]. To lower the energy demand of the O2 separation with MIEC mem­branes, the required high temperatures for the process can be supplied through the reuse of waste heat from high-temperature industries, i.e., steelworks and glassworks.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: d high temperatures for the process can be supplied through the reuse of waste heat from high-temperature industries, i.e., steelworks and glassworks. A partial pressure difference of oxygen along the membranes can be achieved through air flow on the feed side of the membranes, together with the removal of oxygen on the permeate side. This can be attained via vacuum, which requires electrical energy, or via oxygen-free sweep gases. Once these conditions are met, the O2 mole­cules are adsorbed on the membrane surface, capture negative electrical charges and are split into oxygen ions O2-. The ions are subsequently diffused through the membrane wall from the feed side to the permeate side. Simultaneously, electrons move in the opposite direction, main­taining charge neutrality. On the permeate side, the reverse surface re­action occurs, and the oxygen ions are recombined into oxygen molecules and desorbed either through vacuum or a sweep gas [24]. At Fraunhofer IKTS in Hermsdorf, Germany, different plants from laboratory setups to small-scale industrial units were developed using the vacuum process. In 2017, the total energy demand was reduced to <0.72 kWh/m3 STP. Especially the low electricity demand of 0.2 kWh/ m3 STP compared to the total thermal energy demand of 0.52 kWh/m3 STP argues for very low operational expenditures of the process.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: demand of 0.2 kWh/ m3 STP compared to the total thermal energy demand of 0.52 kWh/m3 STP argues for very low operational expenditures of the process. Nevertheless, the usage of sweep gases can further reduce the electrical energy demand and eliminate the requirement of a vacuum pump, which has high investment and maintenance costs. Recently, several oxygen-free sweep gases were researched, for example, carbon dioxide [25–27], argon [28–30], or helium [27,31,32]. However, steam is particularly advantageous as a sweep gas due to its low cost and high condensation temperature, simplifying the separation of pure oxygen [33]. This study describes how Fraunhofer IKTS fabricated the BSCF 5582 membrane tubes used in the experiments. The study also presents the design and operation of the experimental setup and outlines compre­hensive experimental results along with a performance evaluation under varying boundary conditions. Finally, results from long-term experi­ments are provided to demonstrate the durability of BSCF 5582 in steam environments. 2. Experimental setup In the following, the preparation method of the samples is presented in subsection 2.1. The design and the functionality of the experimental setup are detailed in subsection 2.2, while the employed measurement devices are described in subsection 2.3. Finally, subsection 2.4 explains the course of the conducted measurements. 2.1.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: hile the employed measurement devices are described in subsection 2.3. Finally, subsection 2.4 explains the course of the conducted measurements. 2.1. Preparation of the samples Monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ (BSCF 5582) tubes were manu­factured from a powder synthesized with standard solid-state synthesis. The initial stoichiometric amounts of BaCO3, SrCO3, FeOOH, and Co3O4 were mixed without additives in a compact mixer B53 (beba Technol­ogy), working on simultaneous vessel rotation and powder agitation at different degrees of intensity. The mixture was later calcined at 1100 ◦C for 6 h. After calcination, the sample was crushed and coarse-milled with conventional equipment consisting of a standard jaw crusher and a disc mill (FRITSCH GmbH). Fine milling was also carried out in a powder bed jet mill (NETZSCH-Ger¨atebau GmbH) to achieve an average particle size of 2–3 μm. To improve kneading and extrusion behavior, the powder was sub­jected to another annealing step at 700 ◦C for 10 h. The annealed powder was subsequently mixed with water and specific amounts of organic additives (cellulose ethers and polyethers) and kneaded in a twin-shaft kneader (Hermann Linden Maschinenfabrik GmbH & Co. KG) to obtain plastic behavior. The kneaded mass had a water content ranging from 10 to 12 wt% and it was extruded in mass form by a single-screw vacuum extruder (Brabender, 19/32 aspect ratio).

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: mass had a water content ranging from 10 to 12 wt% and it was extruded in mass form by a single-screw vacuum extruder (Brabender, 19/32 aspect ratio). An extrusion die was used to produce tubes with a green outer diameter of 12.25 mm and a green inner diameter of 9.75 mm. One end of each green tube was immediately sealed after extrusion by a customized adaptation tool, forming a sealed end. The tubes were later cut into lengths of approxi­mately 1 m and slowly dried over wooden rods under plastic sheeting to ensure a uniform removal of water. Finally, the green tubes were sintered at 1080 ◦C for 2 h in air. The sintering program included an initial debinding stage at temperatures below 500 ◦C under reduced oxygen partial pressure. The resulting monolithic BSCF 5582 tubes with one closed end had an outer diameter of ~10 mm, a thickness of ~1 mm and were cut to a final length of ~700 mm for later testing. 2.2. Structure and functionality of the experimental setup Fig. 1 presents a CAD model of the developed experimental setup for O2 separation experiments with steam as sweep gas, with the most important components numbered and labeled. Before the start of the measurement, all components in contact with steam are preheated.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: gas, with the most important components numbered and labeled. Before the start of the measurement, all components in contact with steam are preheated. The membrane tubes (4) are heated via the furnace (5) to the desired temperature (700 ◦C–900 ◦C) at a heating rate of 5 K/ min, while the piping of the experimental setup is heated via a heating cord to approximately 150 ◦C to prevent condensation of steam at first exposure. The evaporator (1) can be supplied with water from either the water container [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \varphi}{\partial x^2} = -F \sum_{i=1}^{4} z_i C_i\tag{12}\end{equation}] or the condenser (8). However, at the beginning of the measurement, the condenser (8) is empty. Therefore, the connection between the condenser and the evaporator remains closed. Thus, the water container is initially filled with 600–700 mL of distilled water. The generated steam flows through the outer pipe of the double-pipe heat exchanger (2) into the steam distributor (3). A cross-section of the steam distributor is illustrated in Fig. 2, left. Due to the closed ends of the membrane tubes, the steam distributor must also function as a steam collector. Thus, the steam distributor is designed with two levels. In the bottom level, the steam is distributed into nine welded stainless-steel pipes, each positioned inside a mem­brane tube.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: signed with two levels. In the bottom level, the steam is distributed into nine welded stainless-steel pipes, each positioned inside a mem­brane tube. These pipes have an outer and inner diameter of 6 mm and 4 mm, respectively, creating an annular gap of approx. 2 mm between them and the membrane tubes. The steam flows through the stainlesssteel pipe and is channeled via the sealed end of the membrane tubes into the annular gap. To achieve the O2 partial pressure difference across the membranes, clean, dry air is fed inside the furnace around the

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: ASU>0.4620,000–300,00099.5PSA>0.90.01–50095VPSA>0.34100–500095 K. Khajryan et al. Journal of Membrane Science 740 (2026) 124939 membrane tubes. The volumetric flow of air is controlled via the mass flow controller (MFC) (6) and is preheated to approximately 350 ◦C using a heating cord (7). With both conditions for the O2 permeation of the membrane tubes established, the O2 molecules are transferred from the outer air flow through the membrane into the inner steam flow. The resulting mixture of steam and O2 flows through the annular gap back to the upper level of the steam distributor (3). From there, the mixture is directed into the inner pipe of the double-pipe heat exchanger (2), which preheats the newly generated pure steam by precooling the mixture before it arrives at the condenser (8). A 3D cross-sectional view of the condenser is represented in Fig. 2, right. Inside the spiral pipe of the condenser, cooling water flows, with its inlet temperature of 20 ◦C regulated by a thermostat. This ensures the nearly total condensation of steam, achieving the separation of the oxygen from the steam-oxygen mixture.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: regulated by a thermostat. This ensures the nearly total condensation of steam, achieving the separation of the oxygen from the steam-oxygen mixture. The separated oxygen flows through the mass flow meter (MFM) [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_i}{\partial x} + R_i, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{10}\end{equation}] and O2-analyzer [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} C_i \frac{\partial \varphi}{\partial x}, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{11}\end{equation}] to measure both its flow rate and purity. The O2 purity was measured using two different devices. The primary O2 analyzer from FoxSense AG offers a very high accuracy (±0.1 %) but is not suitable for very humid gas flows. Therefore, a handheld device from NRC International GmbH was also utilized despite its lower measure­ment accuracy (±1 %). Nevertheless, both devices delivered aligned measurements. Due to the closed connection between the condenser (8) and the evaporator (1), the condensed water remains in the condenser. The collected condensed water assists with the condensation of the steam, as the oxygen-steam mixture bubbles through it.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: er remains in the condenser. The collected condensed water assists with the condensation of the steam, as the oxygen-steam mixture bubbles through it. Once approxi­mately 600 mL of water has accumulated in the condenser (8), the aforementioned connection is opened and the water container [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \varphi}{\partial x^2} = -F \sum_{i=1}^{4} z_i C_i\tag{12}\end{equation}] is disconnected from the system by a shut-off valve. Therefore, the condensed water flows back to the evaporator (1), facilitating a closedcycle process. The attachment of the membrane tubes to the upper surface of the stainless steel steam distributor has consistently posed a challenge due to the difference in thermal expansion coefficients α between the stainless steel 1.4841 (αSteel = 18 * 10-6 K-1) and BSCF 5582 (αBSCF =18–22 * 10-6 K-1 for the temperature range 20–800 ◦C [34]) particu­larly at high temperatures. In previous measurements [20], a heat-resistant ceramic adhesive was employed to solve this issue. However, the gas-tightness of the adhesive declined at high tempera­tures. Moreover, the rigidity of the adhesive-based attachment method hindered the replacement of defective membrane tubes. In the present measurements, PTFE-sealed fittings were utilized as an alternative to the adhesive (see Fig. 2, left), ensuring better flexibility and maintaining gas tightness under high-temperature conditions. 2.3.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: s an alternative to the adhesive (see Fig. 2, left), ensuring better flexibility and maintaining gas tightness under high-temperature conditions. 2.3. Measuring devices and boundary conditions Various parameters were investigated to assess the performance of MIEC membranes with steam as a sweep gas. Experimental measure­ments were conducted at furnace temperatures tFurn ranging from 700 ◦C to 900 ◦C, in increments of 50 K. The mass flow rate of steam ˙mSt was varied between 200 g/h, 300 g/h and 500 g/h. Each measurement lasted a minimum of 7 h, during which the furnace temperature and mass flow of steam were maintained at constant values. The volumetric flow rate of air ˙VAir was increased gradually in steps of 4 L/min, covering a range from 0 to 12 L/min. Each air flow rate was held constant for at least 1 h, except for 8 L/min, which was maintained for at least 4 h. The active length of the membrane tubes was approximately 500 mm, providing a total active area of ~1413 cm2. In contrast to previous studies [20], the outer diameter of the membrane tubes was used instead of the inner diameter for the calculation of the total active area to improve compatibility with other scientific publications, for example [21,22]. The relative humidity of the resulting O2 flow was measured using a temperature/humidity probe (Testo SE & Co. KGaA).

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: blications, for example [21,22]. The relative humidity of the resulting O2 flow was measured using a temperature/humidity probe (Testo SE & Co. KGaA). The specific hu­midity x (kgH2O/kgO2) was calculated using the measurements and Equation 1: x = RO2 RH2O φps(tO2)p -φps(tO2)(1) where RO2 = 259.8 J/(kgK) and RH2O = 461.5 J/(kgK) are the specific gas constants of oxygen and water vapor respectively, ps(tO2) is the saturation pressure of water vapor at given temperature tO2 (Pa). Assuming that the mixture is composed solely of oxygen and water vapor, the mass fraction of water vapor ξH2O (kgH2O/kg), the mass fractions of oxygen ξO2 (kgO2/kg) and the volume fraction of water vapor ψH2O (vol%) were calculated using standard Equations 2, 3 and 4: ξH2O =xx + 1(2)

content: Fig. 1. CAD model of the experimental setup with the main components: (1) evaporator with an integrated water pump (ADROP Feuchtemesstechnik GmbH), (2) double-pipe heat exchanger, (3) steam distributor, (4) nine monolithic BSCF 5582 membrane tubes, (5) furnace (Carbolite Gero GmbH), (6) mass flow controller for the air flow (Bronkhorst), (7) heating pipe for the air flow, (8) condenser, (9) cooling water inlet and outlet, [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_i}{\partial x} + R_i, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{10}\end{equation}] pressure controller and mass flow meter for the separated O2 flow (Bronkhorst), [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} C_i \frac{\partial \varphi}{\partial x}, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{11}\end{equation}] O2 analyzer (FoxSense AG) and [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \varphi}{\partial x^2} = -F \sum_{i=1}^{4} z_i C_i\tag{12}\end{equation}] water container with a shut-off valve. The components (2) and (7) are wrapped with heating cords (Hillesheim GmbH) with a power of 125 W/m. The piping is insulated with a 15 mm thick glass wool needled mat.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: Fig. 2. Left: cross-section of the steam distributor illustrating the flow of steam inside the membrane tubes attached Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) sealed fittings– right: 3D cross-sectional view of the condenser displaying the rele­vant flows. K. Khajryan et al. Journal of Membrane Science 740 (2026) 124939 ξO2 = 1 -ξH2O(3) ψH2O =ξH2O /MH2OξH2O /MH2O + ξO2 /MO2 * 100 %(4) where MH2O = 18 kg/kmol and MO2 = 32 kg/kmol are the molar masses of water vapor and oxygen respectively. Table 2 states the measurement accuracy of the measuring devices used. The volumetric flow rate of the separated oxygen ˙VO2 was recorded at 5-s intervals under the experimental parameters. At lower tempera­tures and steam mass flow rates, the measured values displayed some fluctuations. To reduce these fluctuations and improve data clarity, a moving average was calculated for every 24 data points (equivalent to 2 min). 2.4. Measuring process To illustrate the complete course of the measurements, the results from two test conditions (condition 1: tFurn = 900 ◦C and ˙mSt = 300 g/h, condition 2: tFurn = 700 ◦C and ˙mSt = 200 g/h) are presented in Fig. 3. At both conditions in Fig. 3, it was observed that despite the absence of forced air flow, an average O2 separation of approximately 750 mL/ min was achieved at condition 1 and around 270 mL/min at condition 2. The measured O2 purity was also approx. 96 vol%.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: ration of approximately 750 mL/ min was achieved at condition 1 and around 270 mL/min at condition 2. The measured O2 purity was also approx. 96 vol%. This phenomenon can be attributed to air being drawn via natural convection, driven by the significant temperature gradient between the furnace temperature (700 ◦C or 900 ◦C) and the surrounding environment (approx. 30 ◦C). After approximately 2 h from the start of each experiment, the setup was switched to a closed-cycle process, as described in subsection 2.2. The transition always caused a temporary drop in the volumetric flow rate of the separated O2. Nevertheless, the flow rate stabilized after a brief period of 3–5 min back to the previous value (see Fig. 3). Notably, at condition 2 (Fig. 3, right), an increase in the forced air flow rate had a negligible impact on the O2 volumetric flow rate, which remained fluctuating within the range of 250–290 ml/min. Similar behavior was observed in all experiments conducted at 700 ◦C and 750 ◦C. This could be explained by the full occupation of the available free spaces in the perovskite structure of the membrane tubes by the O2 from the natural air flow. The fluctuations observed are likely due to the low furnace temperatures of 700 ◦C or 750 ◦C, which are below the optimum activation threshold of the membrane tubes (850 ◦C). In contrast, the O2 flow rate at condition 2 (Fig. 3, left) is more stable due to the introduction of 4 L/min of forced air flow.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: rane tubes (850 ◦C). In contrast, the O2 flow rate at condition 2 (Fig. 3, left) is more stable due to the introduction of 4 L/min of forced air flow. However, this did not cause a significant increase in the average O2 output. A notable rise in the O2 flow rate, from 750 mL/min to 790 mL/min, was observed only when the air flow rate was increased to 8 L/min. Further increases in air flow did not lead to substantial differences in O2 output. Nevertheless, the approximate 5 % increase in O2 output when doubling the air flow rate suggests that the influence of the air flow rate is minimal in this experimental setup. For a better investigation of the impact of air flow in future experiments, the design of the experimental setup should be modified to ensure the furnace is airtight, preventing the intake of additional (false) air.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: 3. Results and discussion In the following, the results of the conducted measurements are represented. In subsection 3.1, the effect of the improved air-tightness via PTFE-sealings compared to a previous study [20] is described. Subsection 3.2 exhibits the effect of the mass flow rate of steam, while subsections 3.3 and 3.4 describe the purity of the resulting O2 flow and the energy demand of the process, respectively. Finally, subsections 3.5 and 3.6 show the results of the long-term measurements. 3.1. Effect of the new attachment method of the membrane tubes As described in subsection 2.2, the attachment method of the membrane tubes was modified. The heat-resistant adhesive previously employed in published measurements [20] was replaced by more airtight PTFE-sealed fittings, since the temperature of the attachment position is < 250 ◦C. To evaluate the improvements introduced by this new attachment method, Fig. 4 compares the experimental results under identical conditions (tFurn = 700–900 ◦C, ˙mSt = 300 g/h and ˙VAir = 8 L/min). As demonstrated in Ref. [20], a linear relationship between the furnace temperature and the separated O2 flow rate was observed using the heat-resistant adhesive, with higher O2 separation at higher furnace temperatures. This linear correlation persisted with the switch to PTFE-sealed fittings.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: sistant adhesive, with higher O2 separation at higher furnace temperatures. This linear correlation persisted with the switch to PTFE-sealed fittings. However, the improved airtightness of the PTFE-sealed fittings resulted in an upward shift of the trendline with an average of approximately 72 mL/min at each temperature. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 4, the slope of the trendlines using both attachment methods remained consistent under identical experimental conditions. It is noteworthy that the measurement at tFurn = 650 ◦C was not repeated with the PTFE-sealed fitting, as the separated O2 flow rate at this tem­perature was very low and exhibited significant fluctuations in the previous measurements [20]. 3.2. Effect of the steam mass flow To exhibit the effect of the mass flow of steam, the moving averages of the O2 flow rates achieved with different steam mass flows in the entire furnace temperature range are presented in Fig. 5. In these measurements, the air flow rate was 8 L/min. In addition, the averaged O2 flow rates of the final hour of each test condition are plotted in Fig. 6. By comparing the measurements for the different steam mass flows (see Fig. 5), it is evident that the O2 flow rate becomes more stable at higher furnace temperatures and increased steam mass flow rates. Additionally, Fig. 6 shows that a higher steam mass flow rate leads to a proportionally greater O2 output. This can be attributed to the increasing oxygen partial pressure difference.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: higher steam mass flow rate leads to a proportionally greater O2 output. This can be attributed to the increasing oxygen partial pressure difference. With a higher steam mass flow, the oxygen produced is more rapidly transported away from the membrane, causing the partial pressure of oxygen to a greater decrease relative to the air on the opposite side of the membrane. An average increase in the O2 volume flow rate of approximately 47 % was observed when the steam mass flow rate was increased from 200 g/h to 500 g/h (a 150 % increase). Table 3 shows the changes in the O2-flow rate caused by the increase in the mass flow of steam. The O2 flux across the membranes ranged from 0.182 mL/(cm2min) to 0.673 mL/(cm2min), with the highest value obtained at 900 ◦C and a steam mass flow rate of 500 g/h. These values can be found in Table 4. In a previous study employing the same experimental setup, the maximum recorded O2 flux was 0.5 mL/(cm2min) [20] at the same temperature but with a lower steam mass flow rate of 300 g/h, related to the outer surface of the membrane. This indicates that enhancing the attachment method (see subsection 3.1) of the membrane tubes and increasing the mass flow rate of steam have resulted in an improved O2 flux. However, the observed O2 flux remains lower than the 1.35 mL/(cm2min) re­ported by Zhang et al. [12] utilizing monolithic 1 mm thick BSCF disc

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: Table 2 The deviation limit of the implemented measuring devices in the experi­mental setup DeviceDeviation Temperature sensors in ◦C±0.004tMFC in L/min±(0.005 ˙V + 0.02)MFM in mL/min±(0.005 ˙V + 2)O2 Analyzer (FoxSense) in vol%±0.1O2 Analyzer (NRC) in vol%±1Relative humidity probe in %±2 K. Khajryan et al. Journal of Membrane Science 740 (2026) 124939 membranes with argon as a sweep gas at 900 ◦C. The reduced flux in this study could be attributed to the interaction of H2O molecules with the membrane surface, potentially hindering the desorption of oxygen compared to other sweep gases. Similar effects were reported in other studies, where morphological changes [13] and the formation of car­bonates of Ba and Sr [35] were observed on the membrane surface. Furthermore, Bucher et al. [36] demonstrated that the use of argon with increased relative humidity led to a significant decrease in the oxygen permeation of La0.6Sr0.4CoO3-δ compared to dry argon. Nevertheless, steam remains a favored sweep gas option due to its comparatively high condensation temperature, facilitating efficient separation of high-purity oxygen and lower costs. 3.3. Purity of the O2 flow The oxygen purity at all test conditions remained consistently in the range of 95–99 vol% throughout the measurements, with no recognizable dependence on the varied process parameters. The tem­perature in the condenser had the greatest influence on oxygen purity.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: ents, with no recognizable dependence on the varied process parameters. The tem­perature in the condenser had the greatest influence on oxygen purity. This temperature is influenced by several factors, such as the pumping power of the air-cooled thermostat, ambient temperature or the condensed water level in the condenser (see Fig. 2, right). Table 5 shows a summary of the O2 purity at each test condition. Most of the remaining impurities are likely non-condensed water vapor, as the resulting flow was highly humid. To validate this assumption, the relative humidity of the O2 stream was measured with a humidity/temperature probe. The relative humidity φ of the O2 flow across all testing conditions ranged from 33 % to 97 % at a constant absolute pressure p of 1 bar, with the temperature of the flow tO2 varying between 23 ◦C and 30 ◦C. The calculated values for the volume fraction of water vapor are presented in Table 5. By comparing the values of O2 purity and the volume fraction of water vapor in Table 5, the assumption that the remaining impurities in the O2 flow are primarily uncondensed water vapor can be validated. The sum of the volume fractions at all measuring points does not add up to 100 %. However, this can be explained by the measurement uncertainties. 3.4. Energy demand of the process To calculate the energy demand of the process, only the evaporation enthalpy of water and the subsequent superheating of steam to 150 ◦C are considered.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: calculate the energy demand of the process, only the evaporation enthalpy of water and the subsequent superheating of steam to 150 ◦C are considered. Heating the membrane tubes to the investigated tem­perature occurs during the start-up phase of the process. During oper­ation, the membrane tubes must be maintained at this temperature. Therefore, the associated energy demand is to compensate for the thermal losses. However, these losses are negligible compared to the energy required for steam evaporation. The thermal energy input is therefore calculated using Equation 5: ˙Q = ˙mSt⋅(ΔhEvap + ΔhSH )(5) where ˙Q is the required thermal energy (W), ˙mSt is the mass flow of steam (kg/s), ΔhEvap = 2258 kJ/kg is the evaporation enthalpy of water at an absolute pressure of 1 bar, ΔhSH = 96 kJ/kg represents the enthalpy increase for the superheating of steam to 150 ◦C at 1 bar. Therefore, the energy demands are approx. 131 W, 196 W and 327 W for the steam mass flow rates of 200 g/h, 300 g/h and 500 g/h, respectively. The specific energy requirement per liter of oxygen was determined across the full measuring range using the calculated values and the measured O2 flow rates in Fig. 6. These results, shown in Fig. 7, indicate

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: At a steam flow rate of 500 g/h, the setup achieves a higher O2 yield but at the expense of energy efficiency, as indicated by the highest volume-specific energy requirement. In contrast, the lowest specific energy consumption was observed at a steam mass flow rate of 200 g/h, demonstrating the highest efficiency. This can be attributed to the fact that increasing the steam mass flow rate from 200 g/h to 500 g/h (equivalent to +150 %) only results in an approx. 47 % average increase in O2 flow rate (see Table 3). The lowest calculated energy requirement was 11.6 kJ/L O2 (equivalent to 3.22 kWh/m3 STP) at a furnace temperature of 900 ◦C and a steam mass flow rate of 200 g/h. While this value is high compared to established technologies, e.g., ASU and PSA (see Table 1), the flow rate of the experimental setup (max. 960 L/min, equivalent to 0.058 m3/h) is significantly lower than that of these methods. Therefore, a direct comparison without upscaling the experimental setup is not conclusive. Simply increasing the number of membrane tubes can increase the O2 output without increasing the steam mass flow. Furthermore, the cur­rent configuration does not yet incorporate integrated heat recovery from the waste heat of the experimental setup. One promising oppor­tunity lies in the recovery of the latent heat of steam condensation. This could be used to partially or fully re-evaporate the condensed water.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: oppor­tunity lies in the recovery of the latent heat of steam condensation. This could be used to partially or fully re-evaporate the condensed water. To enable this, the steam must condense at an elevated pressure (e.g., 2 bar) and the resulting condensate could then be throttled back to 1 bar to achieve a temperature gradient of approximately 20 K between condensation and evaporation. However, this approach requires addi­tional electrical energy to compress the steam flow, but a theoretical full recovery of the condensation enthalpy would reduce the specific energy demand of the experimental setup to 0.9 kJ/L O2 (equivalent to 0.25 kWh/m3 STP). Such a reduction would improve the energy performance of the experimental setup, bringing it into the range of fully developed oxygen production technologies, despite its lower production scale. This highlights the significant potential of membrane-based systems when optimized for heat integration.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: The results are shown in Fig. 8. During the first half hour of the measurement, the O2 flow rate is very fluctuating. In this period, the purity of the flow is also relatively low (50–70 vol%). This can be explained by the presence of air in the piping of the experimental setup at the start of the measurement. Therefore, the air must first be displaced by the steam flow and this leads to a fluctu­ating flow with relatively low O2 concentration. At the 2-h mark, the dip in the O2 flow rate is a result of switching to a closed steam cycle, as explained for the measurements shown in Fig. 3. In the first 11 h of the measurement, a slight gradual increase can be observed. However, for the remainder of the measurement, the O2-flow rate stays stable. The average O2 flow rate was 693 mL/min in the first 12 h, whereas in the second half, it was 701 mL/min. Furthermore, the O2 purity was constant throughout the measurement at approx. 96 vol%. These results demonstrate that the experimental setup was stable for extended measurement periods. 3.6. Long-term durability of BSCF 5582 To evaluate the long-term stability of BSCF 5582, the material was analyzed independently of the experimental setup within a controlled environment. A polished BSCF 5582 disc was partially covered with a segment from another BBSF 5582 disc, joined by silver (Ag) solder to create a sealed interface. The exposed half of the sample was kept in a continuous steam flow at 850 ◦C for 24 days.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: , joined by silver (Ag) solder to create a sealed interface. The exposed half of the sample was kept in a continuous steam flow at 850 ◦C for 24 days. Afterward, the previously exposed surface was also sealed using Ag solder and another disc segment. The entire sample was then embedded in resin, cross-sectioned at the specific regions of interest, and prepared through standard grinding and polishing steps. Microstructural and compositional ana­lyses were performed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The results of the EDX analysis are shown in Fig. 9, whereas the SEM analysis is presented in Fig. 10. The EDX and SEM analyses of the BSCF disc reveal no significant difference in the elemental composition between the samples with and without steam exposure at 850 ◦C for 24 days. Additionally, the line scan in Fig. 10 shows that no corrosion layer has formed, indicating that BSCF 5582 is stable under these conditions. The small amount of Ag detected within the BSCF 5582 region, as seen in Fig. 10, is a result of surface contamination caused by grinding and polishing of the cross-sectioned sample. However, despite the stability of BSCF 5582 in a steam atmosphere, in a different experimental setup, the steam flow can dissolve unwanted elements or components from other system parts, such as stainless-steel pipes, insulation and sealing materials.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: the steam flow can dissolve unwanted elements or components from other system parts, such as stainless-steel pipes, insulation and sealing materials. These particles can be trans­ported by the steam flow and subsequently deposited on the surface of the membrane tubes. Over time, these deposits can reduce the O2 permeation of the membranes. In the present study, after 85 h of oper­ation, chromium-rich deposits were detected on the inner surface of the membrane tubes. The source of these deposits is likely the used 1.4841 stainless-steel pipes, which were implemented inside the membrane tubes for steam transport. Fig. 11 presents SEM images comparing the surfaces of the affected membrane tubes (right) with a reference mem­brane that was not exposed to steam (left). To determine the effect of steam on the permeation rate of BSCF 5582, a test was carried out using a disc with a thickness of 1.7 mm with steam as sweep gas. The results of this investigation, presented in Fig. 12, revealed a 17 % reduction in O2 permeation after five days of testing. Initially, the material reached a maximum permeation rate of approximately 1.45 mL/(cm2min) STP within one day. The decrease was caused by clearly visible silicate deposits on the surface of the disc (see disc in Fig. 12). These deposits could have originated from silicatecontaining materials within the experimental setup, such as sealing materials, hoses and glass components. However, as demonstrated by Liu et al.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: icatecontaining materials within the experimental setup, such as sealing materials, hoses and glass components. However, as demonstrated by Liu et al. [37], silicon originating from the raw material can diffuse from the bulk of the membrane towards the surface, especially on the sweep side of the membrane. This forms a layer of silicon which hinders the

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content:
Fig. 8. O2 flow rate during the 24-h measurement - tFurn = 850 ◦C, ˙mSt = 300 g/ h and ˙Vair = 8 L/min. K. Khajryan et al. Journal of Membrane Science 740 (2026) 124939 oxygen permeation. 4. Conclusion and outlook This study demonstrated the feasibility of using monolithic BSCF 5582 membrane tubes for oxygen separation with steam as a sweep gas using a designated experimental setup at different operating parameters. Thereby, nine membrane tubes were investigated with an outer diam­eter of 10 mm and an active length of 500 mm each in the temperature range of 700–900 ◦C. On the feed side of the membranes, preheated air was deployed and on the permeate side, steam was used as a sweep gas with mass flow rates from 200 g/h to 500 g/h. Applying PTFE-sealed fittings significantly improved gas tightness and operational flexibility, leading to an average increased O2 yield of approx. 72 mL/min compared to the previous heat-resistant adhesive-based method [20]. The experimental results showed consistently high oxygen purity (95–99 vol%) and a clear dependence of oxygen flux on furnace tem­perature and steam flow rate. The maximum performance was observed

Fig. 9. Qualitative representation of the results of the EDX analysis of a BSCF 5582 disc, left: without steam exposure – right: after 24 days of steam exposure, without an active O2 separation.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: Fig. 10. Line scan and results of the SEM analysis of a BSCF 5582 disc, left: without steam exposure – right: after 24 days of steam exposure, without an active O2 separation. K. Khajryan et al. Journal of Membrane Science 740 (2026) 124939 at 900 ◦C and a steam flow of 500 g/h, resulting in an O2 flow of 960 mL/min at 99 vol% purity. Increasing the mass flow rate of steam would increase the O2 output; however, this was not possible with the current experimental setup. The impurities were proven to be uncondensed water vapor. While the highest oxygen flow rate was achieved at elevated steam flow rates, the energy demand analysis revealed that the best efficiency was found at the lowest steam mass flow rate of 200 g/h. This indicates an inverse correlation between throughput and energy consumption. Importantly, the consistent oxygen purity (max. 99 vol%) underlines the practical viability of the process. Although the O2 flux remains below values reported for alternative sweep gases like argon [12], the high condensation temperature of steam makes it a suitable option for industrial deployment. Further optimization, particularly the integration of heat recovery (e.g., via latent heat of condensation), could significantly reduce the specific en­ergy demand from 3.22 kWh/m3 STP to a theoretical minimum of about 0.25 kWh/m3 STP and bring the technology closer to the efficiency levels of fully established large-scale oxygen generation methods, such as PSA or ASU.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: 25 kWh/m3 STP and bring the technology closer to the efficiency levels of fully established large-scale oxygen generation methods, such as PSA or ASU. Therefore, it is essential to integrate internal and external waste heat sources to generate the required steam flow, which would improve the energy efficiency of the process and reduce the operational costs and environmental impact compared to the other fully established methods. Furthermore, BSCF 5582 showed chemical stability under steam conditions at 850 ◦C, with no detectable corrosion or changes in the chemical composition observed after 24 days of continuous expo­sure. However, the study also revealed that steam can transport different chemical components from other system parts, leading to deposition on the membrane surface and a decline in O2 permeation. In particular, chromium-rich and silicate-based deposits were detected as deposed particles. These findings highlight the importance of choosing the sys­tem components carefully and taking preventive measures to protect the membranes during long-term operation. Future work should focus on upscaling the membrane module, investigating higher mass flow rates of steam, improving furnace airtightness to eliminate false air inflows, better investigating the effect of the air flow rate, and experimentally validating heat integration strategies.

citation.title: Oxygen production using monolithic Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ membranes and steam as sweep gas

content: tness to eliminate false air inflows, better investigating the effect of the air flow rate, and experimentally validating heat integration strategies. Furthermore, longer measurements are necessary to better examine the long-term stability and effectiveness of the membrane tubes, especially in a steam atmosphere. These steps are essential to demonstrate the full potential of MIEC membrane-based oxygen sepa­ration using steam as a sweep gas for decentralized O2 production for industries with abundant waste heat.
